feed_card.title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
feed_card.author: Daniel Kahneman
feed_card.one_line: A Nobel-winning psychologist maps the two systems behind your judgments, and shows where the fast one fails.
teasers: The first number named usually wins the negotiation
Losing $100 hurts twice as much as gaining $100
Why do experts fall for the very traps they study?

sections.content: Most books about cognitive bias borrow their findings from other people's laboratories. This one comes from the psychologist who, with Amos Tversky, ran the experiments that built the field, and it gathers forty years of that work into a single vocabulary. By the end you can put a name to the exact way a judgment went wrong.

content.quote: The intuitive System 1 is more influential than your experience tells you, and it is the secret author of many of the choices and judgments you make.
A reliable way to make people believe in falsehoods is frequent repetition, because familiarity is not easily distinguished from truth.
We can be blind to the obvious, and we are also blind to our blindness.

content.best_for: Readers who want a vocabulary for the exact ways their own judgment goes wrong

sections.content: System 1 is always on. It reads a face, finishes a familiar sentence, and hands you an answer before you have noticed there was a question, all without any feeling of effort. System 2 is the part you think of as yourself, the deliberate reasoner that does arithmetic and weighs options and can override the snap answer. It is also slow and easily tired, and it tends to wave through whatever System 1 has already proposed. Kahneman's claim is that System 1 carries most of the load most of the time, and that the shortcuts making it superb for survival also breed a long catalogue of errors once they run into probability, statistics, and money. Those errors are systematic. They recur in the same shapes across different people and situations, which is what makes them possible to study and to name. The book maps the result: where intuition can be trusted, where it cannot, and why even the experts who know the research are not protected from it.

content: Part one introduces the two systems, System 1's automatic impressions and System 2's effortful control, and lays out how attention and mental effort behave.
From there Kahneman shows why intuition has no feel for statistics, working through anchoring, availability, representativeness, and regression to the mean.
Expertise gets its own long stretch, where he argues that confident forecasts and expert intuition are often illusions assembled from coherent stories.
Prospect theory then takes over, with reference points, loss aversion, the endowment effect, and the way an option's framing settles the choice before you notice.
At the close, Kahneman splits the self that lives through an experience from the self that remembers it, and asks which one our choices should serve.

content.title: Two systems share the work, and the fast one runs the show
content.body: Look at a photograph of a furious face and you grasp her mood at once, with no sense of having done anything. Now multiply 17 by 24. The answer does not arrive on its own, your pulse climbs, and you feel the work. Those are the two modes. The first, System 1, is automatic and fast and never switched off, the source of impressions and intuitions. The second, System 2, is the slow, deliberate reasoner we tend to identify with as ourselves. What the book keeps showing is that System 1 originates most of what we end up thinking and choosing, while the reasoning self we are so proud of mostly signs off on whatever System 1 hands it.
content.in_practice: Treat any judgment that arrives effortless and certain as a reason to slow down and check it.

content.title: When a question is hard, the mind quietly answers an easier one
content.body: A fund manager asks himself whether to buy Ford stock, and without noticing it he answers a different question: do I like Ford cars? The hard question, about a company's future, gets swapped for an easy one, about a feeling, and the answer to the easy question stands in as if it had settled the hard one. This is substitution, the engine behind most intuitive judgment. Asked how satisfied you are with your life, you may report how cheerful you feel this minute. Asked to judge a stranger's competence, you may register how strong his jaw looks. The swap is invisible from the inside, which is why it usually goes uncorrected.
content.in_practice: Catch a fast answer and ask whether it addresses the real question or a simpler cousin you swapped in.
content.quote: When faced with a difficult question, we often answer an easier one instead, usually without noticing the substitution.

content.title: Confidence measures the story's neatness, not its truth
content.body: Give people only one side of a legal case and they reach firmer conclusions than people who hear both sides, even when everyone knows half the evidence is missing. Kahneman's name for it is WYSIATI, what you see is all there is: System 1 builds the most coherent story it can from whatever is in front of it and stays untroubled by the gaps. Because the story holds together it feels true, and the confidence it produces measures the neatness of the story far more than the evidence behind it. A witness who knows little can sound more certain than one who knows a great deal, and the same effect runs on our own beliefs from the inside.
content.in_practice: Before trusting any confident conclusion, hunt for the evidence that is missing, since a tidy story is what produces the confidence in the first place.
content.quote: Declarations of high confidence mainly tell you that an individual has constructed a coherent story in his mind, not necessarily that the story is true.

content.title: The first number you meet drags every estimate toward it
content.body: Tversky and Kahneman rigged a wheel of fortune to land only on 10 or 65, spun it in front of students, then asked what share of UN members are African nations. Those who saw 65 guessed 45 percent on average. Those who saw 10 guessed 25 percent. A spin of a wheel tells you nothing about Africa, yet it moved the answers by 20 points. Anchoring is the name for it: any number you weigh before estimating becomes a starting point, and the adjustment away from it almost always stops too soon. A high asking price makes a house feel more valuable, the first salary figure named in a negotiation tends to win, and the effect holds even on experts who can see it being done to them.
content.in_practice: In any negotiation, fix your own number before you hear theirs, and treat their opening figure as noise that is trying to anchor you.

content.title: A detail that fits the story makes it feel likelier and less probable at once
content.body: Linda is 31, single, outspoken, a former philosophy student who once marched against nuclear weapons. Which is more probable: that she is a bank teller, or that she is a bank teller who is active in the feminist movement? Most people choose the second, though every feminist bank teller is also a bank teller, so the narrower description cannot be the more likely one. The mistake has a name, the conjunction fallacy, and behind it sits representativeness, the habit of judging probability by how well a description fits a stereotype while ignoring the plain arithmetic of sets. Each detail that suits the picture, the feminism, the marching, makes the story richer and more convincing, and every one of them can only lower the odds.
content.in_practice: Distrust the detailed, specific scenario that feels more likely than the vague one, because each added condition narrows the odds further.

content.title: A loss hurts about twice as much as the same gain feels good
content.body: Offered a coin flip that wins $150 or loses $100, most people refuse, even though the math favors taking it. The sting of the possible loss outweighs the pull of the larger possible gain. Across many such experiments the ratio holds steady: a loss feels roughly 1.5 to 2.5 times as strong as an equivalent gain. Kahneman and Tversky built this into prospect theory, which says we register outcomes as gains and losses measured from where we currently stand, with losses always looming larger. There is an evolutionary logic to it, since a creature that treats threats as more urgent than opportunities is the one that survives to reproduce, and the same asymmetry governs how we price our homes, cling to losing stocks, and resist any change framed as giving something up.
content.in_practice: Reframe any choice that comes to you as a loss, and ask whether you would still decide the same way if the identical outcome were dressed as a gain.

content.title: Reword the very same facts and the choice reverses
content.body: A surgeon can describe one operation two ways. 'The one-month survival rate is 90 percent' reassures patients. 'There is 10 percent mortality in the first month' alarms them, even though the two sentences carry identical information. In the most famous version, a disease is expected to kill 600 people. A program that saves 200 for certain is preferred to a gamble, while the same program described as letting 400 die for certain gets rejected in favor of the gamble. That is a framing effect: logically equivalent descriptions produce different feelings and different choices, because each of us usually sees only one version and takes it for the whole truth. When Kahneman handed the disease problem to public health officials whose decisions set real policy, they fell for the wording the same way the students had.
content.in_practice: Restate any important option in its opposite frame, gain into loss or survival into mortality, and see whether your preference survives.

content.title: The self that remembers overrules the self that lived
content.body: Two patients undergo a colonoscopy and rate their pain every 60 seconds. One has a short procedure that ends on a sharp spike. The other has one lasting three times as long but tapering to a mild finish, and that second patient, who objectively suffered more, remembers it as the easier ordeal. Kahneman splits the experiencing self, which lives through each moment, from the remembering self, which scores the whole episode afterward by its peak and its ending and pays no attention to how long it lasted. We choose by the remembered version, and so we return to whatever left the better memory, even when it cost more in the living than the alternative would have.
content.in_practice: Hold apart how good an experience is to live through from how good it will be to remember, in judging the past and in planning the future.

content.body: Kahneman's own team once predicted their textbook would take two years to finish. It took eight, and one member already knew that around 40 percent of teams like theirs never finished at all. They had built the forecast from the inside, imagining their own particular path to success while ignoring the dismal record of projects like theirs. Kahneman's prescription is the outside view. Before you commit to an estimate of time or cost or odds of success, stop describing your specific case, find a reference class of similar past efforts, look up how those turned out, and start your estimate from that base rate, adjusting only for what is genuinely unusual about yours. A team planning a kitchen renovation should begin from the average overrun of real renovations, which in one survey came in at more than double the expected cost, and treat its own hopeful sketch with suspicion. The group version of the same move is Gary Klein's premortem. Before a plan is locked in, you gather everyone, say 'imagine it is a year from now and this has failed', and have each person write the story of that failure, which surfaces the doubts that optimism and politeness would otherwise bury. What both habits share is the move to judge a decision by how well it was made, looking past how it happened to come out.

content.question: What distinguishes System 1 from System 2 in Kahneman's account?
options: System 1 is fast and automatic; System 2 is slow and effortful
System 1 is conscious reasoning; System 2 is unconscious instinct
System 1 handles language; System 2 handles vision
System 1 is rational; System 2 is emotional
content.explanation: System 1 produces quick, automatic impressions with no sense of effort, while System 2 is the slow, deliberate mode that can check them but tires easily.

content.question: What does WYSIATI, 'what you see is all there is', describe?
options: People can hold only one visual object in mind at a time
Memories fade unless they are visually vivid
The mind judges using only the information in front of it and ignores what is missing
Experts notice more detail in their field than novices do
content.explanation: WYSIATI names System 1's habit of building a confident story from whatever evidence is available while staying blind to the gaps.

content.question: According to Kahneman, what happens during substitution?
options: A first impression is corrected once more evidence arrives
A hard question is unconsciously replaced by an easier related one
A memory is replaced by a more flattering version of itself
System 2 takes over a task that System 1 cannot handle
content.explanation: When a question is hard, System 1 swaps in an easier substitute, often answering how something feels in place of what is true, and reports that as the original answer.

content.question: Which situation best illustrates the anchoring effect?
options: You remember a vivid plane crash and overestimate the risk of flying
You assume a quiet stranger is shy because the description fits
You feel a $100 loss more sharply than a $100 gain
A high sticker price makes a lower offer on the same car feel like a deal
content.explanation: Anchoring is when an initial number sets a reference that drags later judgments toward it, so the inflated sticker price quietly shapes what counts as a good price.

content.question: A flight instructor concluded that praise hurt his cadets and criticism helped. What did he miss?
options: Extreme performances tend to be followed by more average ones whatever the feedback
Cadets resent praise and respond better to fear
Praise makes people overconfident and careless
Criticism carries more useful detail than praise
content.explanation: An unusually good or bad performance is usually followed by a more typical one on its own, so the instructor credited his feedback for what was really regression to the mean.

content.question: Across experiments, how much heavier does a loss feel than an equivalent gain?
options: About ten times as heavy
Exactly the same, once wealth is accounted for
Roughly 1.5 to 2.5 times as heavy
It varies so much that no consistent ratio exists
content.explanation: Prospect theory finds a stable asymmetry: losing feels about 1.5 to 2.5 times as strong as an equivalent gain, which is why people refuse favorable-looking bets.

content.question: Why does Kahneman argue that framing effects undermine the standard economic picture of rational choice?
options: Rational agents should always prefer a sure thing to a gamble
A stable preference should not reverse when the same outcome is only reworded
Rational people should weigh losses and gains equally
Economic models assume people never make arithmetic mistakes
content.explanation: If logically identical descriptions flip a choice, the underlying preference cannot be the stable, reality-based one that rational-choice models assume.

content.question: A patient with a longer, more painful colonoscopy remembered it as easier than a patient with a shorter one. What broader lesson does Kahneman draw?
options: Pain is entirely subjective and cannot be measured at all
Longer procedures are always remembered as more painful
The experiencing self and the remembering self always agree in the end
We judge episodes by their peak and end, so we may repeat what was worse to live through
content.explanation: Memory scores an episode by its peak and its end and neglects how long it lasted, so the remembering self can favor an option that cost the experiencing self more total pain.

sections.content: The book became the standard popular gateway to the heuristics-and-biases and prospect-theory program, the route by which a wide general and professional audience first met that work. Its System 1 and System 2 split turned into common shorthand, used well beyond psychology to name the difference between a fast, automatic reaction and a slow, deliberate one. Applied fields took up its framing, most directly in nudge-style policy design, and more broadly in how medicine and finance talk about judgment under uncertainty. A reader today who hears a colleague mention anchoring, loss aversion, or thinking fast and slow is leaning on a vocabulary this book did much to make ordinary.

content.body: As a young officer in the Israeli army in the 1950s, Kahneman designed a structured interview to screen recruits and watched his confident predictions miss, his first brush with what he later called the illusion of validity. The research the book rests on came out of his decades-long partnership with Amos Tversky, begun at the Hebrew University in the late 1960s. The two wrote every sentence together and produced the studies of heuristics, biases, and prospect theory that the book gathers up. Tversky died in 1996, so when the 2002 Nobel in economics arrived for that work, shared with the experimental economist Vernon Smith, Kahneman received it without him, since the prize is not awarded posthumously. He is regularly described as the first psychologist to win the economics prize.
content.image_attribution: nrkbeta, "Daniel Kahneman", CC BY-SA 3.0 (Wikimedia Commons), cropped

sections.content: The book's broad framework, the two systems and the central findings on judgment and choice, has held up well. One strand has not. In a chapter on priming, Kahneman presented social-priming studies suggesting that subtle cues steer behavior outside awareness, and wrote that 'disbelief is not an option.' The headline example held that people exposed to words about old age then walked more slowly down a corridor, an effect sometimes called the Florida effect. After 2011, independent teams repeatedly failed to reproduce that result and many like it, part of the wider replication crisis that ran through psychology. In 2012 Kahneman wrote an open letter warning priming researchers that he saw 'a train wreck looming', and in 2017, after a detailed analysis concluded the cited studies carried almost no replicable signal, he conceded the point: 'What the blog gets absolutely right is that I placed too much faith in underpowered studies.' He even noted the irony that his own first paper with Tversky, back in 1971, had warned against this very trust in small samples. The concession is to his credit, but it does not repair the chapter: a stretch of a hugely influential book leans on results that have not held up, and that part of it should be read with real caution.

content.label: Reconstruction of a Train Wreck: How Priming Research Went off the Rails, with Kahneman's 2017 reply (Replicability-Index)
Freeman Dyson, How to Dispel Your Illusions (New York Review of Books, 2011)
Tversky and Kahneman, Judgment under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases (Science, 1974)
